pattern: With at most 13 nodes there are only a few thousand possible subsets, so every subset can be checked directly: a cheap parity test on its node values first, then a connectivity check only for the subsets that pass.
hints: There are at most 2^13 subsets of nodes, which is small enough to look at every one of them individually rather than reasoning about the graph structure abstractly.
For each subset, first check the parity of the sum of its node values -- that is a single pass over the subset and instantly rules out about half of them before doing any graph work.

For the subsets that pass the parity check, verify connectivity by walking the induced subgraph (only edges whose both endpoints are inside the subset) from any one of its nodes and confirming every other chosen node was reached.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, a single node with an odd value
a single node with an even value, its own subset always counts
the maximum of 13 nodes forming a complete graph
two isolated nodes with no edge: the pair is never counted even though its value sum is even
two separate disconnected components, each internally connected
a triangle where every node has value 1, so only the pairs and the full triple have even sums, and connectivity must still be checked for each
a star graph: the center connects every subset containing it, but leaf-only subsets without the center are never connected

stress.description: random graphs at the full 13-node scale with random edge density and random 0/1 node values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 13)
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)]
    possible_edges = [(u, v) for u in range(n) for v in range(u + 1, n)]
    rng.shuffle(possible_edges)
    m = rng.randint(0, len(possible_edges))
    edges = [list(e) for e in possible_edges[:m]]
    return [nums, edges]
